ks1.objective: Dribble around the area while keeping the football away from Spider-Man's web. If your ball is hit, do a simple challenge before rejoining.
successCriteria: Keeps the ball moving rather than standing still
Looks up to see where Spider-Man is
Completes the challenge calmly before returning

lks2.objective: Dribble with control under pressure, using awareness to avoid the tagger. Complete a ball-mastery challenge cleanly to re-enter.
successCriteria: Changes direction when Spider-Man closes in
Keeps the ball close without freezing
Completes the re-entry task before charging back in

uks2.objective: Combine scanning, body shape and tight control to evade a faster tagger. Treat the re-entry challenge as a mini-reset, not a penalty.
successCriteria: Uses fakes or disguised touches under pressure
Performs the re-entry task with quality, not haste

space.description: A square playing area sized for the group. All children take a football into the area. Cones mark the boundary. One child is chosen as Spider-Man — they hold a rolled-up, tied bib as their 'web'.

value.name: Resilience
value.text: Resilience through bouncing back. Getting hit by Spider-Man's web isn't a failure — it's a chance to do something well before rejoining. The fastest re-entry is the cleanest one, not the rushed one. Praise the calm response after being hit, not just avoiding the web.

steps: Mark a square area with cones. All children take a football into the area.
Choose one child to be Spider-Man. Give them a rolled-up, tied bib — this is their 'web'.
Spider-Man attempts to shoot their web to hit a dribbling child's football.
If a child's ball is hit, they must complete a challenge before rejoining the game.
Spider-Man retrieves the web after each throw and continues. Rotate Spider-Man regularly.
progressions: Re-entry challenge: 5 toe taps on the ball.
Re-entry challenge: jump over the football 5 times.
Re-entry challenge: 10 'penguin kicks' (instep taps, alternating feet).
Re-entry challenge: kick the ball into your hands 3 times.
Re-entry challenge: dribble to a target goal and score before rejoining.
questions: How did you decide which direction to go when Spider-Man got close?
Did being hit feel different the second or third time? Why?
Which re-entry challenge made you slow down the most? Why is that a good thing?

step.space: Smaller area = more traffic + more hits; larger area = more room to evade.
step.task: Give Spider-Man more webs (2 or 3 rolled bibs) to increase the speed of the game. Top performers: weaker foot only, specific surfaces only.
step.equipment: Add target goals for the score-a-goal re-entry challenge. Varying ball sizes for younger children.
step.people: Have two Spider-Men working together for older or larger groups.

base.showmanship: Use the superhero theme to capture imagination. Cast a confident child as Spider-Man first to set the energy. Make 'web-slinging' sounds. Praise calm focus shown when completing challenges — 'That's superhero composure right there!'
